subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Statement 2 is the classic trap — IGC membership is not required for countries to trade in grains; it is an advisory/intergovernmental body, not a trade bloc requiring membership.
elimination_hint: India IS a member of IGC (Statement 1 correct). IGC does not mandate membership for grain trade — countries freely export/import wheat and rice without being members (Statement 2 wrong).
factual_hooks: International Grains Council (IGC) is an intergovernmental commodity body monitoring global grain markets
IGC membership is voluntary and not a prerequisite for grain trade — India is a member
contrast_with: IGC (monitoring/advisory) vs WTO (binding trade rules requiring membership for export/import regulations)
contemporary_relevance: India's wheat export ban (2022) and rice export restrictions were discussed in IGC forums.

subject: History
trap_factor: Students may guess Durga Puja (2021 inscription) as more recent or Kumbh Mela (2017) — Garba dance was inscribed in December 2023, making it the latest.
elimination_hint: Timeline: Kumbh Mela (2017), Chhau dance (2010), Durga Puja (2021), Garba dance (December 2023) — Garba is the most recent addition to UNESCO ICH list.
factual_hooks: Garba dance of Gujarat was inscribed on UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage list in December 2023
India now has 15+ elements on UNESCO's ICH list including Yoga, Kumbh Mela, Durga Puja
contrast_with: UNESCO World Heritage Sites (tangible) vs Intangible Cultural Heritage (practices, traditions)
contemporary_relevance: Garba's UNESCO recognition has boosted cultural tourism in Gujarat and raised awareness of living heritage.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Students often confuse Rajendra Prasad (permanent President) with the provisional president; C. Rajagopalachari is a well-known name that may distract.
elimination_hint: Dr. Sachchidananda Sinha was the oldest member of the Constituent Assembly and therefore became its Provisional President on 9 December 1946. Dr. Rajendra Prasad was elected permanent President on 11 December 1946.
factual_hooks: Dr. Sachchidananda Sinha served as Provisional President of Constituent Assembly (Dec 9, 1946)
Convention: oldest member presides as provisional chair — Sinha was the eldest member
related_concepts: B.R. Ambedkar as Drafting Committee Chairman
contrast_with: Provisional President (ceremonial first session role) vs Permanent President (elected, presided over all sessions)

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement 2 is the trap — Defence and Foreign Affairs were kept under the Viceroy/Governor-General (reserved subjects), NOT the federal legislature. Students confuse reserved vs federal subjects.
elimination_hint: GoI Act 1935 did provide for an All India Federation (Statement 1 correct). But Defence and External Affairs were reserved subjects of the Governor-General, not the federal legislature (Statement 2 wrong).
factual_hooks: GoI Act 1935 envisaged All India Federation of British provinces and Princely States — never fully implemented
Defence and Foreign Affairs remained with Viceroy under reserved subjects — not transferred to legislature
contrast_with: GoI Act 1919 (dyarchy in provinces) vs 1935 Act (provincial autonomy + proposed federation)

subject: History
trap_factor: Natyashastra (Bharata Muni) and Mahabhashya (Patanjali) are famous Sanskrit works that may mislead; Kavyalankara is by Bhamaha — none are Bhasa's works.
elimination_hint: Bhasa was a Sanskrit playwright (pre-Kalidasa era). His works include Svapnavasavadattam, Madhyama-vyayoga, Pancharatra etc. Madhyama-vyayoga is the correct answer.
factual_hooks: Bhasa was an ancient Sanskrit playwright; 13 of his plays were rediscovered by T. Ganapati Shastri in Kerala (1912)
Madhyama-vyayoga is a one-act play by Bhasa based on the Mahabharata story of Bhima and Ghatotkacha
contrast_with: Natyashastra (Bharata Muni — treatise on dramaturgy) vs Bhasa's actual plays

subject: History
trap_factor: All four properties are UNESCO World Heritage Sites of India — students may not know the specific years of inscription. Rani-ki-Vav (2014) and Mahabodhi Temple (2002) are older inscriptions.
elimination_hint: Shantiniketan and Sacred Ensembles of the Hoysalas were both inscribed in 2023. Rani-ki-Vav was inscribed in 2014, Mahabodhi Temple in 2002. Only two (Shantiniketan + Hoysalas) are from 2023.
factual_hooks: Shantiniketan (Rabindranath Tagore's university) inscribed as UNESCO WHS in September 2023
Sacred Ensembles of Hoysalas (Belur, Halebidu, Somnathapura) also inscribed in September 2023
prerequisite_concepts: UNESCO World Heritage Sites of India
contrast_with: 2023 inscriptions (Shantiniketan, Hoysalas) vs earlier inscriptions (Rani-ki-Vav 2014, Mahabodhi 2002)
contemporary_relevance: India now has 42 UNESCO World Heritage Sites after 2023 inscriptions.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Students may think 'Repeal' is not permitted under Article 368 as it seems to conflict with Basic Structure doctrine — but Article 368 text explicitly includes repeal as a method.
elimination_hint: Article 368 verbatim states Parliament may amend by way of 'addition, variation or repeal' — all three are correct. Answer is D. Note: Repeal of Basic Structure features is still constitutionally impermissible despite the text.
factual_hooks: Article 368 uses the words 'addition, variation or repeal' as methods of constitutional amendment
Kesavananda Bharati case (1973) held that Parliament cannot use Article 368 to repeal/abrogate Basic Structure
contrast_with: Article 368 procedure (special majority) vs ordinary legislation (simple majority)

subject: Society
trap_factor: Nigeria and South Africa have high and growing populations — they are not known for low birth rates or ageing. Italy, Japan, and South Korea are the classic demographic decline cases.
elimination_hint: Italy (TFR ~1.2), Japan (ageing, shrinking population), South Korea (world's lowest TFR ~0.72) are the demographic crisis countries. Nigeria and South Africa have young, growing populations.
factual_hooks: South Korea has world's lowest TFR (~0.72 in 2023), Japan is world's most aged society
Italy and Japan have some of the fastest-shrinking working-age populations in the world
contrast_with: Italy/Japan/South Korea (low TFR, ageing) vs Nigeria/South Africa (young, high growth)
contemporary_relevance: South Korea's demographic crisis and Japan's immigration debate are key global demographic policy issues.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Students may include the Home Minister of India (option 4) as a member after 2002 amendment — but the Home Minister was NOT added; the amendment added nominated members.
elimination_hint: After 2002 amendment, NEC comprises: Governors and Chief Ministers of constituent states (1,2) and three nominated members by President (3). Home Minister of India is NOT a member — select 1, 2, 3 only.
factual_hooks: NEC was set up under NEC Act 1971 for development of North-East; 2002 amendment restructured it
After 2002 amendment, NEC has: 8 Governors, 8 CMs, + 3 Presidential nominees — Home Minister excluded
contrast_with: NEC (multi-state development council) vs Zonal Councils (advisory inter-state coordination)
contemporary_relevance: NEC is key coordinating body for India's Act East Policy and Northeast infrastructure development.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Students may answer 'Three' (1952, 1963, 1973) missing the fourth Delimitation Commission of 2002. Four commissions have been constituted in total.
elimination_hint: Delimitation Commissions: 1st (1952 Act), 2nd (1963 Act), 3rd (1973 Act), 4th (2002 Act) — four commissions in total up to December 2023.
factual_hooks: Four Delimitation Commissions constituted: 1952, 1963, 1973, and 2002
Delimitation determines constituency boundaries; 4th Commission (2002) report implemented before 2008 elections
contrast_with: Delimitation (boundary redrawing) vs Delimitation freeze (seats fixed till 2026 under 42nd Amendment)
contemporary_relevance: 5th Delimitation Commission is expected after 2026 Census for women's reservation implementation.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Maithili is the trap — it was added by the 92nd Constitutional Amendment (2003), not the 71st. Students may lump all language additions together.
elimination_hint: 71st Amendment (1992) added Konkani, Manipuri, and Nepali to the 8th Schedule. Maithili was added later by the 92nd Amendment (2003). So answer is 1, 2, 3 only (A).
factual_hooks: 71st Constitutional Amendment (1992) added Konkani, Manipuri, Nepali — taking 8th Schedule to 18 languages
92nd Amendment (2003) added Maithili, Dogri, Bodo, Santhali — taking total to 22 languages
contrast_with: 71st Amendment (1992, 3 languages) vs 92nd Amendment (2003, 4 more languages)
contemporary_relevance: Demands for inclusion of more languages (Bhojpuri, Rajasthani) in 8th Schedule are ongoing.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Inter-State trade, migration are confused as State subjects; Corporation tax sounds like a State revenue. Inter-State quarantine being a Union subject is the least intuitive correct answer.
elimination_hint: Union List Entry 81: Inter-State migration; Entry 79: Inter-State quarantine. Corporation Tax is a Union subject (Entry 85). Inter-State trade is a Union/Concurrent subject. Answer C is correct.
factual_hooks: Union List Entry 28: Inter-State quarantine — disease control across state boundaries is Central subject
Corporation tax is in the Union List — not a State subject despite being collected from companies
prerequisite_concepts: 7th Schedule — Union, State and Concurrent Lists
contrast_with: Inter-State quarantine (Union List) vs Public health (State List Entry 6)
contemporary_relevance: COVID-19 highlighted the Union's quarantine powers vs State's public health jurisdiction tensions.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Students may guess Article 19 (freedom of speech/expression) as privacy relates to personal liberty and free speech, but the Supreme Court placed privacy under Article 21.
elimination_hint: K.S. Puttaswamy judgment (2017) unanimously declared Right to Privacy as a fundamental right under Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty).
factual_hooks: K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017): 9-judge bench held Right to Privacy is intrinsic to Article 21
Privacy is protected as part of 'personal liberty' under Article 21 — not a separate article
contrast_with: Article 19 (explicit listed freedoms) vs Article 21 (implied rights including privacy)
contemporary_relevance: India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act (2023) flows directly from the Right to Privacy under Article 21.

subject: Internal Security
trap_factor: Statement 2 says CDS exercises military command over Service Chiefs — this is the biggest trap; CDS is NOT in the chain of command over Service Chiefs operationally.
elimination_hint: CDS is Permanent Chairman of COSC (1 correct) and Principal Military Advisor to Defence Minister on tri-service matters (3 correct). CDS does NOT exercise operational command over Service Chiefs (2 wrong). Answer is D (1 and 3).
factual_hooks: CDS created in January 2020 is Permanent Chairman of Chiefs of Staff Committee (COSC)
CDS is Principal Military Advisor to Defence Minister on tri-service matters but NOT in operational chain of command over individual Service Chiefs
contrast_with: CDS (coordination, advisory) vs Service Chiefs (operational command of their respective forces)
contemporary_relevance: India is pursuing Theatre Commands which will eventually place CDS above theatre commanders — reform ongoing.

subject: Internal Security
trap_factor: Operation Maitri (Nepal earthquake relief) and Operation Sankalp (Gulf safety) sound like humanitarian civil operations — Operation Sadbhavana is the specific term for army's civil outreach in J&K and NE.
elimination_hint: Operation Sadbhavana is the Indian Army's long-running humanitarian initiative for upliftment of local population in remote/disturbed areas — primarily J&K and Northeast.
factual_hooks: Operation Sadbhavana launched in 1998 for civil outreach in J&K; extended to NE states
It covers education, health, women empowerment, and infrastructure in remote conflict-affected areas
contrast_with: Operation Sadbhavana (long-term civil uplift) vs Operation Maitri (one-time disaster relief)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may guess Russia-Kazakhstan (vast shared steppe border) or India-China — the Canada-US border (~8,891 km) is the world's longest international land border.
elimination_hint: Canada-US border is 8,891 km (including Alaska) — the longest land border between any two countries. Russia-Kazakhstan is second longest at ~7,644 km.
factual_hooks: Canada-US border at ~8,891 km is the world's longest international land border
Russia-Kazakhstan border (~7,644 km) is the world's second longest international land border
contrast_with: Canada-US (longest, peaceful) vs India-Pakistan (shorter, contested)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 2 says only MPs can complain — this is wrong; any citizen or body can complain through an MP. Statement 3 about sub-judice matters is correctly stated.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 (ad-hoc initially, then permanent in 2000) is correct. Statement 2 is wrong — complaints can come from any person. Statement 3 (sub-judice bar) is correct. Answer: 1 and 3 only (C).
factual_hooks: Lok Sabha Ethics Committee was set up as ad-hoc in 2000, later made permanent
Ethics Committee cannot take up sub-judice matters (pending before courts)
contrast_with: Ethics Committee (unethical conduct) vs Privileges Committee (breach of privilege)
contemporary_relevance: Ethics Committee became prominent after cash-for-query and cash-for-vote cases in Parliament.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 1 says provisions come into effect from 18th Lok Sabha — this is wrong; the act requires delimitation before implementation, making 18th Lok Sabha too early.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is wrong — implementation requires delimitation (post-2026 census), so 18th Lok Sabha cannot have women's reservation. Statements 2 (15 years sunset clause) and 3 (SC women's sub-quota) are correct.
factual_hooks: Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam (106th Amendment) provides 33% reservation for women in Lok Sabha and state assemblies
Implementation requires fresh delimitation post-2026 census; has 15-year sunset clause with SC/ST women's sub-quota
contrast_with: Panchayati Raj women's reservation (already implemented, 1/3 seats) vs Parliament (pending delimitation)
contemporary_relevance: Women's reservation in Parliament (106th Amendment, 2023) is one of the most significant recent constitutional changes.

subject: Internal Security
trap_factor: Exercise Mitra Shakti is between India and Sri Lanka (not Bangladesh); Statement 1 is the key error. Students may confuse it with India-Bangladesh exercise (Sampriti).
elimination_hint: Mitra Shakti is India-Sri Lanka bilateral exercise. Statement 1 is wrong. Statements 2 (Pune, Aundh), 3 (counter-terrorism goal), 4 (IAF participation) are correct. Answer: D (2, 3, 4).
factual_hooks: Exercise Mitra Shakti is India-Sri Lanka annual bilateral military exercise — NOT India-Bangladesh
India-Bangladesh military exercise is called 'Sampriti'; Mitra Shakti 2023 was held at Aundh, Pune
contrast_with: Mitra Shakti (India-Sri Lanka) vs Sampriti (India-Bangladesh)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Students may confuse Prohibition with Certiorari (which quashes orders of lower courts) or with Mandamus. Prohibition specifically stops ongoing proceedings.
elimination_hint: Writ of Prohibition is issued to an inferior court/tribunal to stop proceedings that are beyond its jurisdiction — it prevents, rather than quashes. Answer is C.
factual_hooks: Prohibition writ prevents inferior courts from exceeding jurisdiction — issued BEFORE judgment
Certiorari quashes the order AFTER it is passed; Prohibition stops proceedings midway
contrast_with: Prohibition (stops ongoing proceedings) vs Certiorari (quashes completed order of lower court)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 1 says Governor declares Scheduled Tribes — this is wrong; it is the President who specifies STs by public notification under Article 342, on advice of concerned State Governor.
elimination_hint: Only Statement 2 is correct: a community declared ST in one state need not be ST in another (state-specific lists). Statement 1 is wrong — President specifies STs under Article 342, not the Governor.
factual_hooks: Article 342 empowers the President (not Governor) to specify Scheduled Tribes by public notification
ST lists are state-specific — a tribe may be ST in one state but not another (e.g., Gond is ST in some states)
contrast_with: President specifies STs (Article 342) vs State Legislature amends the list through Parliament
contemporary_relevance: Demand for ST status by various communities (Jat, Gujjar, Pahari) remains a recurring political issue.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 1 says Finance Minister lays Annual Financial Statement 'on behalf of the Prime Minister' — the AFS is laid by Finance Minister on behalf of President (not PM) per constitutional convention.
elimination_hint: Both statements, read carefully, are correct: Statement 1 — Finance Minister lays AFS (convention is on behalf of President via PM's cabinet); Statement 2 — demands for grants require President's recommendation (Article 113). Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Annual Financial Statement (Union Budget) is laid before Parliament under Article 112 by Finance Minister
Article 113: No demand for grant can be made except on recommendation of President — money Bill requirement
contrast_with: Finance Minister's role (presentation) vs President's recommendation (constitutional requirement for demands)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Shashi Tharoor is a prolific author on India's history and politics; students may confuse him with Jaishankar as a foreign policy writer.
elimination_hint: 'The India Way: Strategies for an Uncertain World' (2020) and 'Why Bharat Matters' (2024) are both authored by S. Jaishankar, India's External Affairs Minister.
factual_hooks: S. Jaishankar authored 'The India Way' (2020) and 'Why Bharat Matters' (2024)
These books outline India's strategic autonomy, multi-alignment, and civilizational identity in foreign policy
contrast_with: Jaishankar's 'The India Way' (strategic) vs Tharoor's 'An Era of Darkness' (colonial critique)
contemporary_relevance: Jaishankar's writings define India's current foreign policy doctrine — highly relevant for IR preparation.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may question whether connecting Red Sea to Mediterranean actually makes it a route for Gulf oil to Europe — it does, bypassing the Suez Canal or supplementing it.
elimination_hint: Both statements are correct AND Statement-II explains Statement-I: SUMED (Suez-Mediterranean) pipeline connects Red Sea (Ain Sukhna) to Mediterranean (Sidi Kerir) — enabling Gulf oil bypass of Suez Canal to reach Europe.
factual_hooks: SUMED pipeline (Suez-Mediterranean) is 320 km long, connects Ain Sukhna (Red Sea) to Sidi Kerir (Mediterranean)
SUMED handles ~2.5 million bpd of crude oil — strategic when Suez Canal is congested or blocked
contrast_with: Suez Canal (ship route) vs SUMED (pipeline route) — both connect Red Sea to Mediterranean
contemporary_relevance: Houthi attacks on Red Sea shipping (2023-24) have made SUMED pipeline's alternative role more strategic.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may doubt Statement 2 (no river inflow) thinking the Nile or other rivers drain into it — the Red Sea actually receives virtually no river inflow due to the surrounding arid desert.
elimination_hint: Both statements are correct: Red Sea is in a desert zone with minimal rainfall (Statement 1), and no permanent rivers drain into it (Statement 2) — high salinity results from this.
factual_hooks: Red Sea has one of world's highest salinities (~40 ppt) due to minimal rainfall and no river input
Red Sea is surrounded by Arabian and African deserts; the Nile reaches Mediterranean, not Red Sea
contrast_with: Red Sea (high salinity, no rivers, arid) vs Baltic Sea (low salinity, many river inputs, humid)
contemporary_relevance: Red Sea's strategic importance has grown with Houthi disruptions to global shipping (2023-24).

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Ships using fossil fuels (bunker fuel) are notorious for sulphur emissions — students may select this over power plants.
elimination_hint: Power plants burning fossil fuels (coal, oil) are the largest single source of SO₂ globally according to EPA. Coal combustion releases large amounts of sulphur dioxide.
factual_hooks: Power plants (coal-fired) account for ~65% of US SO₂ emissions per EPA
Combustion of coal releases SO₂ as coal contains sulphur compounds — key cause of acid rain
contrast_with: Power plants (coal SO₂, largest) vs ships (SOx, regulated by IMO MARPOL Annex VI)
contemporary_relevance: India's coal-heavy power sector makes SO₂ emission norms critical under its climate commitments.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Students may choose option B (correct but explains differently) — Statement II (military coups) directly explains Statement I (instability) in the Sahel.
elimination_hint: Both statements are correct AND Statement-II explains Statement-I: Military coups in Mali (2021), Burkina Faso (2022), Niger (2023), Gabon (2023) are the primary reason for Sahel's security crisis.
factual_hooks: Sahel has seen 8+ military coups since 2020: Mali (twice), Guinea, Burkina Faso (twice), Niger, Gabon, Chad
Sahel instability driven by jihadist insurgency (JNIM, ISGS) + governance failures leading to military takeovers
contrast_with: West Africa coup belt (Sahel) vs relatively stable East/Southern Africa
contemporary_relevance: Sahel coups and Wagner Group involvement have major implications for India's Africa policy and UN peacekeeping.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement I (India doesn't export apples from USA) is false — India does import apples from USA; Statement II about GM food approval law is true but doesn't explain Statement I.
elimination_hint: Statement I is false — India DOES import apples from USA (it's one of the largest US apple markets). Statement II is true — India has laws requiring approval for GM food imports. Answer: D (Statement-I incorrect, Statement-II correct).
factual_hooks: USA is one of the top exporters of apples to India — Washington state apples are popular in India
Environment Protection Act and FSSAI regulations require approval for importing GM foods into India
contrast_with: GM food import restrictions (regulatory) vs apple import (permitted, high tariff but allowed)
contemporary_relevance: India-US trade negotiations include discussions on agricultural market access and GM food standards.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Students often think Speaker loses all rights including voting when removal resolution is pending — the Speaker retains right to speak and a casting vote, but must NOT preside.
elimination_hint: When removal resolution is under consideration: Speaker shall not preside (1 correct), but retains right to speak (2 wrong) and can vote in the first instance — not just casting vote (3 wrong). Only Statement 1 is correct.
factual_hooks: Article 181: Speaker shall not preside while removal resolution is being considered; Deputy Speaker presides
Speaker retains right to speak and vote (including first instance vote) during removal proceedings — only presiding is prohibited
related_concepts: No-confidence motion vs removal of Speaker
contrast_with: Speaker during normal proceedings (presides, casts vote only in tie) vs removal proceedings (doesn't preside, can vote in first instance)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 3 (bill with joint sitting notification lapses) is the key trap — such a bill does NOT lapse on Lok Sabha dissolution; it is saved specifically to enable the joint sitting.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 (pending Lok Sabha bill lapses on dissolution) = correct. Statement 2 (passed by LS, pending in RS lapses) = correct. Statement 3 (joint sitting notification bill lapses) = WRONG — it is saved. Answer: B (1 and 2).
factual_hooks: Bills pending in Lok Sabha at dissolution lapse; bills in Rajya Sabha (not passed by LS) don't lapse
A bill notified for joint sitting is specially saved from lapsing — Art 108 protects it across dissolution
contrast_with: Bill pending only in RS (doesn't lapse) vs bill pending in LS (lapses on dissolution)

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement II says EU aims to develop ALL its own clean technology by 2040 — this is incorrect; the EU's goal is carbon neutrality by 2050, and it aims to manufacture 40% (not 100%) of clean tech domestically.
elimination_hint: Statement I (EU Net-Zero Industry Act approved) is correct. Statement II is wrong — EU carbon neutrality target is 2050 (not 2040), and it doesn't aim to develop all clean tech domestically. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: EU Net-Zero Industry Act approved in 2024 to scale up clean technology manufacturing in Europe
EU's carbon neutrality target is 2050; Net-Zero Industry Act targets 40% domestic clean tech manufacturing
contrast_with: EU carbon neutrality (2050) vs UK (2050) vs India (2070)
contemporary_relevance: EU's Net-Zero Industry Act creates competitive dynamics for Indian clean tech exports and solar manufacturing.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Statement I (Venezuela recovered) is clearly false — Venezuela has been in severe economic crisis with massive emigration. Statement II (largest oil reserves) is true.
elimination_hint: Venezuela HASN'T recovered — it has the world's largest humanitarian/emigration crisis in Latin America. Statement I is false. Statement II is true (Venezuela has world's largest proven oil reserves). Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Venezuela has world's largest proven oil reserves (~304 billion barrels) per OPEC data
Over 7.7 million Venezuelans have emigrated — one of world's largest displacement crises despite oil wealth
contrast_with: Venezuela (largest reserves, economic failure) vs UAE/Qatar (large reserves, successful diversification)
contemporary_relevance: Venezuela's crisis illustrates resource curse; its oil reserves are relevant to US sanctions and OPEC+ dynamics.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Statement 1 says Centre provides 100% funding — actually it is funded as a Centrally Sponsored Scheme with some state share (though often described as centrally funded).
elimination_hint: All three statements are correct per the scheme guidelines: 100% central funding (1), cadastral map digitisation (2), and transliteration of Records of Rights (3). Answer: D (all three).
factual_hooks: DILRMP digitises land records including cadastral maps, RoRs, and enables online mutation
Records of Rights transliteration across Indian languages is a key component of the scheme
contrast_with: DILRMP (urban+rural land records) vs SVAMITVA (drone mapping of inhabited rural areas)
contemporary_relevance: Digital land records reduce land disputes, which are 60%+ of India's court cases.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Statement 1 says scheme covers second and third trimesters AND six months post-delivery — the PMSMA actually covers only antenatal care (2nd/3rd trimester), not six months post-delivery.
elimination_hint: PMSMA provides free antenatal care checkups on 9th of every month to pregnant women in 2nd/3rd trimester. It does NOT guarantee post-delivery care (Statement 1 wrong). Statement 2 (private sector volunteers) is correct.
factual_hooks: PMSMA (Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan) provides free ANC on 9th of every month
Private OBGYNs and radiologists can volunteer services at government facilities under PMSMA
contrast_with: PMSMA (antenatal care) vs JSY (cash incentive for institutional delivery)
contemporary_relevance: India's MMR reduction from 254 to 97 per lakh live births partly attributed to PMSMA and JSY.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Statement 4 (family pension to unmarried daughters) is wrong — PM-SYM family pension goes only to spouse. Statement 1 (entry age 21-40) is also wrong — correct age is 18-40.
elimination_hint: PM-SYM correct facts: Entry age 18-40 (not 21-40, so Statement 1 wrong); age-specific contribution (Statement 2 correct); ₹3000/month pension at 60 (Statement 3 correct); family pension only to spouse, not daughters (Statement 4 wrong). Answer: B (2 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: PM-SYM entry age is 18-40 years for unorganised workers earning below ₹15,000/month
PM-SYM guarantees ₹3,000/month pension at age 60; government matches beneficiary contribution
contrast_with: APY (entry age 18-40, all citizens) vs PM-SYM (18-40, only unorganised workers)
contemporary_relevance: PM-SYM addresses social security gap for ~93% of India's unorganised workforce.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Portugal (host country) and France (perennial favourite) are common wrong guesses; Greece winning Euro 2004 was one of football's biggest upsets.
elimination_hint: Greece's Euro 2004 victory in Portugal is considered one of football's greatest upsets — Otto Rehhagel's Greece defeated Portugal in the final.
factual_hooks: Greece won UEFA Euro 2004 in Portugal — one of the biggest upsets in international football
Greece defeated Portugal 1-0 in the final in Lisbon, having also beaten them in the group stage
contrast_with: Euro 2004 surprise (Greece) vs Euro 2008 (Spain's dominance began)

subject: Society
trap_factor: Brazil is often cited as populous — but it ranks 7th, behind Pakistan and Bangladesh. USA (3rd) and Indonesia (4th) follow China and India in decreasing order.
elimination_hint: World population order: China, India, USA, Indonesia, Pakistan, Brazil. After China and India, USA (3rd) and Indonesia (4th) follow. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: World population ranking: China (~1.41B), India (~1.43B), USA (~335M), Indonesia (~278M)
Note: India surpassed China as most populous country in 2023
contrast_with: Most populous (India, China) vs fastest growing (Sub-Saharan Africa)
contemporary_relevance: India surpassed China as world's most populous nation in 2023 — a landmark demographic milestone.

subject: History
trap_factor: Gandhi is the obvious guess given his prominence at Karachi session; but Nehru drafted the resolution which reflected socialist economic thinking.
elimination_hint: Jawaharlal Nehru drafted the Karachi Resolution on Fundamental Rights and Economic Programme (1931) — it reflected his socialist vision and was a precursor to constitutional rights.
factual_hooks: Karachi Resolution (1931) drafted by Nehru guaranteed civil liberties, free primary education, and economic rights
This resolution is considered a forerunner to the Directive Principles of State Policy in the Constitution
contrast_with: Gandhi (mass politics, moral framework) vs Nehru (drafted socioeconomic rights framework)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement 3 (pigs must be culled to prevent spread) is the classic misconception — Swine Flu is human-to-human transmission and culling pigs doesn't prevent spread.
elimination_hint: Swine Flu (H1N1 influenza A) spreads human-to-human; antibiotics are ineffective (viral disease). Culling pigs is NOT required for human epidemic control. Only Statement 2 is correct.
factual_hooks: Swine Flu (H1N1) is caused by influenza A virus — antibiotics have no role; antivirals like oseltamivir are used
H1N1 spreads human-to-human via respiratory droplets — pig culling doesn't control human epidemics
contrast_with: Swine Flu (H1N1, no pig culling needed) vs Avian Flu (H5N1, poultry culling recommended)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Statement 1 (no charter) is actually wrong — Commonwealth does have a Charter (adopted 2013). Statement 2 is also wrong — membership is not automatic for all former British territories.
elimination_hint: Both statements are wrong: Commonwealth adopted a Charter in 2013 (Statement 1 incorrect). Former British territories must apply and be accepted — several never joined (e.g., USA, Myanmar) (Statement 2 wrong). Answer: D (neither).
factual_hooks: Commonwealth of Nations adopted its Charter in 2013, affirming shared values
USA, Myanmar, Sudan, and several ex-British territories are NOT Commonwealth members — membership is not automatic
contrast_with: Commonwealth (voluntary, value-based) vs Francophonie (French language-based grouping)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Statement 1 reverses the roles (Commission represents EU in trade; Council is legislators) — but actually Commission represents EU, while European Council (heads of state) sets strategy, and Council of EU legislates. Both statements have errors.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is partially wrong (says Commission represents EU in trade = correct; but says Council 'participated in legislation' understating its role). Statement 2 is completely wrong — European Commission is NOT heads of state. Answer: D (neither as stated).
factual_hooks: European Commission represents EU in external trade negotiations; Council of EU (ministers) legislates
European Council (heads of state) sets strategic direction; European Commission is unelected technocratic body
contrast_with: European Commission (executive, trade rep) vs European Council (strategic, heads of state) vs Council of EU (legislative, ministers)
contemporary_relevance: EU-India FTA negotiations resumed in 2022; understanding EU trade decision structure is relevant.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement C (Ombudsman orders are final and binding) is the incorrect statement — orders are NOT final; banks or complainants can appeal to an Appellate Authority (Deputy Governor, RBI).
elimination_hint: Banking Ombudsman orders are NOT final — appeals can be made within 30 days to Appellate Authority (RBI Deputy Governor). This is the 'not correct' statement. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Banking Ombudsman scheme (RBI) allows free consumer grievance redressal against banks
Awards under Banking Ombudsman can be appealed to Appellate Authority within 30 days — NOT final and binding
contrast_with: Banking Ombudsman (non-judicial, appealable) vs Consumer Forum (judicial, binding after exhaustion)
contemporary_relevance: RBI's Integrated Ombudsman Scheme (2021) merged banking, NBFC, and digital payments ombudsman.

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Students must read carefully — consumption in 1988, not 1987. The two successive percentage changes must be applied correctly in sequence.
factual_hooks: Successive percentage changes: apply first change, then second change to the resulting value
For percentage decrease, multiply by (1 - rate/100); for increase, multiply by (1 + rate/100)
contrast_with: Simple addition of percentages (wrong) vs successive multiplication (correct method)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Astatine and Francium are synthetic/rare elements students may not know well — Tritium (radioactive hydrogen isotope) is well-known. Zirconium is stable and not radioactive.
elimination_hint: Zirconium (Zr, atomic number 40) is a stable transition metal — NOT radioactive. Astatine (radioactive halogen), Francium (radioactive alkali metal), Tritium (radioactive H isotope) are all radioactive.
factual_hooks: Zirconium is used in nuclear reactor fuel cladding precisely because it is non-radioactive and corrosion-resistant
Astatine is the rarest naturally occurring element and radioactive; Francium is the most radioactive naturally occurring element
contrast_with: Zirconium (stable, used in reactors) vs Uranium/Thorium (fissile, radioactive fuel)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Porcupine (spiny rodent) may seem unusual like a primitive egg-layer; Kangaroo is a marsupial (live young, in pouch) — Echidna is the only mammal that lays eggs.
elimination_hint: Echidna (spiny anteater) is a monotreme — one of only five egg-laying mammals (alongside platypus). Kangaroos and whales are placental/marsupial — live young. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Monotremes (platypus and echidna) are the only mammals that lay eggs
Echidna lays a single leathery egg into its pouch; the young (puggle) hatches after 10 days
contrast_with: Monotremes (egg-laying) vs Marsupials (live young, pouch) vs Placental (live young, internal gestation)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may confuse increasing carbon chain with decreasing molecular weight — the order is straightforwardly increasing with carbon number.
elimination_hint: Hydrocarbons molecular weight increases with carbon chain length: Methane (CH₄, 16) < Ethane (C₂H₆, 30) < Propane (C₃H₈, 44) < Butane (C₄H₁₀, 58). Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Alkane series: methane (C1), ethane (C2), propane (C3), butane (C4) — molecular weight increases with carbon number
Each additional CH₂ group adds 14 to molecular weight in the alkane series
contrast_with: Increasing molecular weight (methane→butane) vs decreasing boiling point (butane→methane)

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement 2 (Central Asian tribes persecuted Buddhists) and 3 (Gupta kings opposed Buddhism) are both incorrect — Gupta kings were Vaishnavas but tolerant; Central Asian invaders adopted Hinduism gradually.
elimination_hint: Only Statement 1 is correct: Buddha's absorption into Vaishnavism as an avatar of Vishnu led to Buddhism's intellectual decline. Gupta kings were tolerant (not opposed) and Kushans/Hunas didn't specifically persecute Buddhists.
factual_hooks: Buddha's inclusion as 9th avatar of Vishnu blurred Buddhist-Hindu boundaries, reducing Buddhism's distinct appeal
Gupta period was actually a golden age of Buddhist learning (Nalanda flourished); Guptas were tolerant Vaishnavas
related_concepts: Adi Shankaracharya's role in Buddhist decline
contrast_with: Buddhism's decline in India (absorbed into Hinduism) vs Buddhism's spread to SE Asia and China

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Department of Revenue and Department of Expenditure both sound like they could manage the budget; the Department of Economic Affairs (Budget Division) is the actual unit responsible.
elimination_hint: Department of Economic Affairs (Budget Division) under the Ministry of Finance prepares and presents the Union Budget. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Budget Division of Department of Economic Affairs (DEA) is responsible for Union Budget preparation
DEA also handles capital markets, external assistance, and investment policy alongside budget
contrast_with: DEA (budget preparation, capital markets) vs DoR (tax policy, direct/indirect taxes)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may guess magnesium (red-tinged soils) or phosphates — ferric oxide (iron oxide) is what gives red soil its characteristic colour.
elimination_hint: Red soil gets its colour from ferric oxides (iron(III) oxide / Fe₂O₃) — the same compound that makes iron rust red. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Red soils of Deccan plateau and Eastern Ghats are red due to ferric oxide (Fe₂O₃) content
Red soils are well-drained, low in nutrients but good for dry farming — found in AP, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka
contrast_with: Red soil (ferric oxide, well-drained) vs Black soil (iron, lime, magnesium — retains moisture)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may confuse Gondwana rocks with Deccan traps (limestone and basalt) — Gondwana rocks hold coal, not limestone.
elimination_hint: Gondwana rock system contains over 90% of India's coal reserves — making it the most important rock system for energy. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Gondwana rock system holds ~98% of India's coal reserves — formed from Carboniferous-Triassic period flora
Gondwana coalfields: Damodar valley (Jharia, Raniganj), Sone valley, Mahanadi valley, Godavari valley
contrast_with: Gondwana (coal) vs Vindhyan (limestone, sandstone) vs Deccan Trap (basalt)
contemporary_relevance: Gondwana coalfields are India's primary domestic energy source; coal India Ltd operations are largely here.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may think Luni flows into the Gulf of Kutch or Khambhat given its Rajasthan origin; it actually disappears into the saline Rann of Kutch.
elimination_hint: River Luni originates in Aravalli hills and flows SW through Rajasthan, eventually becoming saline and getting lost in the marshy Rann of Kutch — it does not reach any sea directly.
factual_hooks: Luni river is saline in its lower reaches (from Balotra onwards) — hence 'Luni' means salt in local language
Luni terminates in the Rann of Kutch — an endorheic (internal drainage) basin
contrast_with: Luni (endorheic, lost in Rann) vs Sabarmati (drains to Gulf of Khambhat)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Evergreen oak trees may lead students to think equatorial or humid subtropical climate — Mediterranean climate has evergreen oaks (like cork oak) adapted to dry summers.
elimination_hint: Mediterranean climate: dry hot summers, mild wet winters, evergreen sclerophytic vegetation (cork oak, olive). The three characteristics perfectly match Mediterranean climate. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Mediterranean climate (Cs): dry warm summer, wet mild winter, evergreen oak trees (sclerophytes)
Found around Mediterranean basin, California, Chile (33-45°N/S, west coasts of continents)
contrast_with: Mediterranean (dry summer, wet winter) vs Marine West Coast (year-round precipitation)

subject: Society
trap_factor: Both schemes sound like women's welfare programs; Statement 1 reverses their actual purposes — Swadhar is for distressed/difficult circumstances, not Swayam Siddha.
elimination_hint: Both statements are incorrect: Swadhar targets women in difficult circumstances; Swayam Siddha is for holistic empowerment through SHGs. Both statements' descriptions are swapped. Answer: D (neither).
factual_hooks: Swadhar scheme: for women in difficult circumstances (destitute, victims of violence, trafficking, prisoners)
Swayam Siddha: for women's holistic empowerment through Self Help Groups (SHGs) and convergence
contrast_with: Swadhar (distressed women rehabilitation) vs Swayam Siddha (SHG-based empowerment)

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Students may divide 24 by 4 to get 6 days (for 1/4 full) — but because the basket doubles each day, working backwards is the correct approach.
elimination_hint: Basket doubles daily. If full on day 24, then half-full on day 23, quarter-full on day 22. So basket is 1/4th full after 22 days. Answer: D (22).
factual_hooks: Doubling problem: if full = day 24, then 1/2 full = day 23, 1/4 full = day 22 (work backwards)
This is exponential growth — each day basket = previous day × 2
contrast_with: Linear filling (divide by 4 = day 6) vs exponential doubling (subtract 2 days = day 22)

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Students may think the answer is simply 3 (ratio of diameters) without realising that rolling a coin around another also adds rotations due to the coin's own revolution.
elimination_hint: When a small coin (diameter d) rolls around a large coin (diameter 3d), it rolls (circumference of large)/(circumference of small) = (3πd)/(πd) = 3 times. Answer: C (3).
factual_hooks: Rolling coin problem: number of rotations = circumference of path / circumference of rolling coin
If diameters are 1:3, smaller coin rotates 3 times to travel the perimeter of the larger coin
contrast_with: Rolling around exterior (3 rotations for 1:3 ratio) vs rolling along straight line (1 rotation per circumference)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may doubt Statement-II's explanation — they may think it's just about temperature, not convection explaining troposphere thickness difference.
elimination_hint: Both statements are correct and Statement-II explains Statement-I: Strong equatorial convection lifts air and heat to greater heights → thicker troposphere (~16-18 km at equator vs ~8 km at poles). Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Troposphere: ~16-18 km thick at equator (intense convection) vs ~8 km at poles (subsidence)
Intense solar heating at equator drives strong convectional currents, pushing tropopause to greater height
contrast_with: Equator (thick troposphere, intense convection) vs Poles (thin troposphere, subsidence, temperature inversion)

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Students may directly divide ₹2.50 by 500 to get 0.5% — but must account for the 2-year period.
factual_hooks: Simple Interest formula: SI = PRT/100; difference in SI = P × ΔR × T / 100
contrast_with: Simple interest (linear) vs compound interest (exponential) — this question uses simple interest

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Students may try to find marks per question — but a simple ratio approach works: 12 questions = 60%, so 100% = 20 questions.
elimination_hint: 12 questions = 60% of marks (full marks each). Total questions = 12 × 100/60 = 20. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: If x questions = 60% of test, total questions = x × (100/60) = x × 5/3
12 × 5/3 = 20 questions total
contrast_with: Direct ratio approach (12/60% = n/100%) vs algebraic approach (both give same answer)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may hesitate about nitrogen compounds from volcanoes — volcanoes do emit nitrogen gases (N₂, NH₃, NOx) though in smaller quantities than SO₂ and CO₂.
elimination_hint: All four — pyroclastic debris, ash/dust, nitrogen compounds, and sulphur compounds — are products of volcanic eruptions. Answer: D (all four).
factual_hooks: Volcanic eruptions emit pyroclastics, ash, SO₂, CO₂, H₂S, HCl, HF, N₂, water vapour
Sulphur compounds (SO₂, H₂S) are major volcanic gases responsible for volcanic smog (vog) and acid rain
contrast_with: Volcanic gases (SO₂, CO₂, N₂, HCl) vs anthropogenic gases (CO₂, NOx from industry)
contemporary_relevance: Volcanic SO₂ emissions can cause short-term global cooling — relevant to climate discussions.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement 2 says Gulf Stream and North Atlantic Drift make the Atlantic COLDER — this is completely wrong; they are warm currents that make North Atlantic WARMER than expected.
elimination_hint: In January, isotherms bend north over warm oceans and south over cold continents (Statement 1 correct). Gulf Stream and N. Atlantic Drift are WARM currents making N. Atlantic warmer — isotherms bend north (Statement 2 wrong). Answer: A (only 1).
factual_hooks: January isothermal maps: isotherms bend northward over warm oceans, southward over cold continents
Gulf Stream and North Atlantic Drift are warm currents — they make NW Europe warmer than expected latitude
contrast_with: Gulf Stream (warm, poleward) vs Labrador Current (cold, equatorward) in N. Atlantic
contemporary_relevance: Gulf Stream weakening (AMOC slowdown) due to climate change could reverse warming effect on Europe.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Ghana is well-known for cocoa but students may not know Côte d'Ivoire (Ivory Coast) tops the world — together they produce ~60% of world's cocoa.
elimination_hint: Côte d'Ivoire (~45% of world production) and Ghana (~15%) are the two largest cocoa producers globally. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana together produce ~60% of global cocoa supply
Côte d'Ivoire is the world's largest cocoa producer; Ghana is second — both in West Africa's cocoa belt
contrast_with: Côte d'Ivoire/Ghana (cocoa) vs Ecuador/Dominican Republic (premium/organic cocoa)
contemporary_relevance: Child labour in West African cocoa farms is a major global supply chain ethics issue.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Gomati vs Ghaghara order is the key confusion — Gomati joins Ganga at Varanasi (upstream/west) while Ghaghara joins further east near Saran district.
elimination_hint: West to East order of Ganga tributaries downstream of Prayagraj: Gomati (joins near Varanasi) → Ghaghara (Saran, Bihar) → Gandak (Hajipur) → Kosi (Katihar). Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Gomati joins Ganga near Varanasi (Saidpur); Ghaghara joins near Chhapra (Saran); Gandak at Hajipur; Kosi at Kursela
These left-bank Himalayan tributaries join Ganga in sequence from west to east
contrast_with: Left bank tributaries (Himalayan — Gomati, Ghaghara, Gandak, Kosi) vs Right bank (Peninsular — Son, Damodar)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may think only one factor (CO₂ dissolved in water forming carbonic acid) explains weathering — but atmospheric oxygen also oxidizes iron minerals, contributing to weathering.
elimination_hint: Both Statement-II (CO₂ dissolves in rainwater, forms carbonic acid — chemical weathering) and Statement-III (O₂ in rainwater causes oxidation of minerals) explain Statement-I. Answer: A (both explain).
factual_hooks: Carbonic acid (H₂CO₃) from dissolved CO₂ weathers limestone and silicate minerals (carbonation)
Dissolved oxygen causes oxidation of iron and other minerals — another form of chemical weathering
contrast_with: Chemical weathering (CO₂, O₂ reactions) vs physical weathering (temperature change, abrasion)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Vosges is in Europe (France-Germany border), not Central Asia; Alps are fold mountains (not block/horst); Appalachians are old fold mountains (correct); Andes are fold mountains (correct). Two correct: rows 3 and 4.
elimination_hint: Row 1: Vosges is in Europe, NOT Central Asia (wrong). Row 2: Alps are FOLD mountains, not block mountains (wrong). Row 3: Appalachians (N. America, fold) = correct. Row 4: Andes (S. America, fold) = correct. Only two rows correct. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Vosges mountains are in France/Germany — block (horst) mountains in Europe, not Central Asia
Alps are fold mountains formed by Alpine orogeny; Appalachians are ancient fold mountains
contrast_with: Fold mountains (Alps, Himalayas, Andes — plate collision) vs Block mountains (Vosges, Black Forest — faulting)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement 2 says water vapour is maximum at poles — this is false; water vapour is maximum near the equator (warm air holds more moisture).
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is correct: water vapour decreases with altitude (colder air holds less moisture). Statement 2 is wrong: water vapour is maximum at tropical/equatorial regions (warm humid air), not poles. Answer: A (only 1).
factual_hooks: Water vapour decreases with altitude — temperature drops with altitude, reducing air's capacity to hold moisture
Tropical regions have maximum water vapour content; polar regions have very dry, cold air
contrast_with: Water vapour maximum (equator/tropics) vs minimum (poles and high altitudes)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: The low annual temperature range could suggest equatorial climate, but equatorial climate has very high rainfall (250+ cm) and no distinct seasons; Marine West Coast has moderate and year-round rainfall.
elimination_hint: Marine West Coast (Cfb): low temperature range due to oceanic influence, year-round precipitation 50-250 cm, no distinct dry season. Fits description exactly. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Marine West Coast climate (Cfb): low temp range, rainfall throughout year, 50-250 cm annual rainfall
Found on west coasts 40-65° latitude: British Isles, Pacific NW USA, New Zealand, southern Chile
contrast_with: Marine West Coast (year-round rain, low temp range) vs Mediterranean (dry summer, wet winter)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement 1 (Coriolis increases with wind velocity) is a common misconception — Coriolis force depends on wind speed (velocity) as well as latitude, so Statement 1 is correct.
elimination_hint: Both statements are correct: Coriolis force = 2mΩv sinφ (increases with wind speed v, Statement 1) and is maximum at poles (sinφ=1) and zero at equator (sinφ=0, Statement 2). Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Coriolis force formula: F = 2mΩv sinφ — proportional to wind velocity (v) and sin of latitude (φ)
Coriolis force is zero at equator (sin 0° = 0) and maximum at poles (sin 90° = 1)
contrast_with: Coriolis (deflection, increases with speed) vs Pressure Gradient Force (drives air, independent of speed)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Tropic of Cancer has exactly 12 hours on equinox, and more than 12 hours on June 21 — students may confuse this with exactly 12 hours. Arctic Circle has 24 hours (midnight sun) on June 21.
elimination_hint: June 21 (summer solstice, N. Hemisphere): Equator = exactly 12 hrs. Tropic of Cancer = more than 12 hrs. Tropic of Capricorn = less than 12 hrs (winter there). Arctic Circle = 24 hrs. Answer: D (2 and 4 — Tropic of Cancer and Arctic Circle).
factual_hooks: On June 21: all latitudes north of equator have more than 12 hours daylight; Tropic of Cancer has ~13.5 hours
Arctic Circle (66.5°N) has 24 hours of daylight (midnight sun) on June 21 summer solstice
contrast_with: June 21 (N. Hemisphere summer) vs December 21 (N. Hemisphere winter — reversed daylight)

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Amazon Basin is the most famous tropical region and students instinctively choose it; the Congo Basin's peatlands were only discovered/measured in 2017.
elimination_hint: Congo Basin in Central Africa houses the world's largest tropical peatland (Cuvette Centrale) discovered in 2017 — stores ~30 billion tonnes of carbon (about 3 years of global fossil fuel emissions).
factual_hooks: Congo Basin's Cuvette Centrale peatland: ~145,500 km², stores ~30 billion tonnes carbon — world's largest tropical peatland
Discovered in 2017 by scientists from University of Leeds — previously unmapped
contrast_with: Amazon (largest tropical forest) vs Congo Basin (largest tropical peatland) — different carbon systems
contemporary_relevance: Congo peatland protection is a top climate priority; its destruction would release massive CO₂ equivalent to 3 years of global emissions.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Students may doubt Statement 3 (bioaccumulation) thinking PFAS affects only exposure rather than accumulating — PFAS specifically bioaccumulate and biomagnify up the food chain.
elimination_hint: All three statements about PFAS are correct: widespread in water/food (1), highly persistent (2 — 'forever chemicals'), and bioaccumulate in organisms (3). Answer: D.
factual_hooks: PFAS are called 'forever chemicals' — highly stable C-F bonds resist degradation in environment
PFAS bioaccumulate in blood, liver, kidneys; detected in polar bears, humans in remote areas
contrast_with: PFAS (synthetic, forever chemicals) vs natural POPs (dioxins from burning) — both bioaccumulate
contemporary_relevance: PFAS regulation is intensifying globally; India lacks specific PFAS standards but is developing them.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Students may not recognize Groundnut as a legume (pea family) because it grows underground — but groundnut (Arachis hypogaea) is indeed a legume/fabaceae.
elimination_hint: All three — Groundnut (Arachis hypogaea), Horse-gram (Macrotyloma uniflorum), and Soybean (Glycine max) — belong to Fabaceae (pea/legume family). Answer: C (all three).
factual_hooks: Groundnut, horse-gram, and soybean are all Fabaceae (legume/pea family) — nitrogen-fixing legumes
Groundnut flowers above ground but fruits (pods) develop underground — still a legume
contrast_with: Legumes (Fabaceae — soybean, groundnut, horse-gram) vs pseudo-cereals (amaranth, buckwheat)
contemporary_relevance: Legume cultivation is promoted for nitrogen fixation and protein security under National Food Security Mission.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Indian Flying Fox IS listed as vermin under Wildlife Protection Act 1972 (for agricultural damage). But it does NOT drink blood — that's vampire bats. It feeds on fruits.
elimination_hint: Statement-I is correct (Indian Flying Fox = vermin under WPA 1972 in some states). Statement-II is incorrect — Indian Flying Fox is a fruit bat (frugivore), not a blood-feeder. Answer: C (I correct, II incorrect).
factual_hooks: Indian Flying Fox (Pteropus giganteus) is a large fruit bat — one of world's largest bats, feeds on fruit
It has been listed as vermin in some states due to damage to fruit orchards, despite ecological role in pollination
contrast_with: Indian Flying Fox (frugivore, Asia) vs Vampire bat (blood-feeder, Latin America)
contemporary_relevance: Flying fox conservation debate: listed as vermin for crop damage vs ecological role as pollinator/seed disperser.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement 3 (insured against inflation by design) is the trap — Digital Rupee (e-rupee) is NOT inflation-proof; it carries the same inflation risk as physical currency.
elimination_hint: Statements 1 (sovereign currency by RBI), 2 (liability on RBI balance sheet), and 4 (freely convertible) are correct. Statement 3 is wrong — e-rupee is NOT inflation-indexed. Answer: D (1, 2 and 4).
factual_hooks: Digital Rupee (e₹) is Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC) — sovereign currency on RBI's liability side
e₹ is freely exchangeable 1:1 with physical cash and commercial bank money — not inflation-protected
contrast_with: CBDC (sovereign, legal tender, inflation risk) vs Stablecoin (pegged to asset) vs Crypto (volatile, not sovereign)
contemporary_relevance: India's Digital Rupee (e₹-W and e₹-R) pilots are ongoing; CBDC policy is a key RBI initiative.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Athlete's Foot (tinea pedis) sounds like a bacterial infection due to sweating/athletic activity — it is caused by fungi (dermatophytes), not bacteria.
elimination_hint: Athlete's Foot (tinea pedis) is a fungal infection caused by dermatophytes (Trichophyton species) — the same group causes ringworm and jock itch. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Athlete's Foot (tinea pedis) is caused by dermatophyte fungi — Trichophyton rubrum most commonly
It thrives in warm, moist environments (locker rooms, pools) — hence associated with athletes
contrast_with: Athlete's foot (fungal) vs Plantar wart (viral — HPV) — both affect feet but different pathogens

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Students may confuse 'father's age in 1938 = 10 times son's age in 1930' — carefully note it is the father's age in 1938 = 10 × son's age in 1930 (not son's age in 1938).
elimination_hint: Let son's age in 1930 = s; father's = 8s. Father in 1938 = 8s+8 = 10s → 8=2s → s=4. Son in 1930=4, Father=32. In 1940: Son=14, Father=42. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Set up two equations: (1) father = 8×son in 1930; (2) father in 1938 = 10×son in 1930
Solving: son's age in 1930 = 4; father's = 32; in 1940 son = 14, father = 42
contrast_with: Age at same time (simultaneous) vs age at different times (sequential equation setup)
